Project.name: n0wss
Project.annotation: n0wss is a Web Secure Socket (WSS) proxy service based on the sendme/iroh technology stack. The goal is to let a local client connect to a remote WSS endpoint and route outbound internet traffic through it so proxy-aware applications can use the tunnel as a generic upstream proxy.
Project.keywords: wss, websocket, secure-websocket, proxy, internet-access, iroh, sendme, nat-traversal

Actors.actor-LocalUser: Runs the client on a local PC and wants selected applications to reach the internet through the remote WSS tunnel.
Actors.actor-RemoteNode: Hosts the WSS endpoint and forwards approved outbound traffic.
Actors.actor-Operator: Maintains configuration, credentials, relay settings, and access policy.

UC-001.Action: Configures an application to use the local proxy endpoint.
UC-001.Goal: To send outbound internet traffic through the remote WSS service.
UC-001.Preconditions: The local client is configured with a reachable remote WSS endpoint and valid access credentials or ticket material.
UC-001.AcceptanceCriteria: A proxy-aware application can complete a remote connection through the tunnel and receive a valid response.

UC-002.Action: Accepts a secure websocket handshake and upgrades it into an active proxy session.
UC-002.Goal: To create an authenticated encrypted tunnel without exposing raw transport details to the end user.
UC-002.Preconditions: The WSS listener is bound, TLS is configured, and admission policy allows the request.
UC-002.AcceptanceCriteria: The session is authenticated, observable, and ready to carry bidirectional traffic.

UC-003.Action: Observes failures and rejects invalid proxy requests.
UC-003.Goal: To diagnose tunnel problems without leaking secrets or raw payloads into logs.
UC-003.Preconditions: Structured logging and stable trace markers are enabled.
UC-003.AcceptanceCriteria: Critical failures can be correlated by session identifiers and sensitive fields remain redacted.

NonGoals.item-1: No general-purpose VPN client in v1; the first target is proxy-aware applications only.
NonGoals.item-2: No anonymous open relay; access control and admission policy are mandatory from the start.
NonGoals.item-3: No browser-based control panel in the initial implementation.

Constraints.constraint-1: Transport must preserve encryption in transit and avoid exposing raw credentials or traffic payloads in logs.
Constraints.constraint-2: The implementation should reuse the sendme/iroh ecosystem where possible instead of replacing NAT traversal and relay behavior from scratch.
Constraints.constraint-3: The first architecture must support generic proxy clients such as SOCKS5 or HTTP CONNECT capable applications.

Risks.risk-1: WSS framing and proxy byte streams may introduce head-of-line blocking or backpressure bugs if bidirectional pumping is not carefully implemented.
Risks.risk-2: Authentication mistakes could turn the remote endpoint into an unintended public proxy.
Risks.risk-3: NAT traversal or relay fallback behavior from the underlying transport may differ between platforms and networks.
Risks.risk-4: Long-lived sessions may leak resources if shutdown and reconnect paths are not verified early.

OpenQuestions.question-1: Should the first client-facing proxy mode be SOCKS5 only, HTTP CONNECT only, or both?
OpenQuestions.question-2: Will the remote WSS endpoint terminate TLS directly or sit behind an existing reverse proxy?
OpenQuestions.question-3: Should authentication be static token based, ticket based, or mTLS based in the first release?
